class-files-and-instances.label: Class Files and Instances
class-files-and-instances.summary: Create a class shell with `pass`, import it from a model file, and make one object cleanly.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.title: Define the Car class shell
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.prompt: In `models/car.py`, define `class Car:` with `pass` inside it. Do not add a constructor yet. Do not add attributes or methods yet. `main.py` can stay unchanged in this first step.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.hint: This step is only about making the class exist in its own file.

help.concept: `pass` means the class exists, but it does not do any work yet. That is enough for the first clean class shell.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.starterCode:
# The class definition belongs in models/car.py.
# You do not need to add code in main.py yet.

main_py.content:
# The class definition belongs in models/car.py.
# You do not need to add code in main.py yet.

models_car_py.content: # Define the Car class shell below.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.solutionCode:
# The class definition belongs in models/car.py.
# You do not need to add code in main.py yet.

main_py.content:
# The class definition belongs in models/car.py.
# You do not need to add code in main.py yet.

0.message: Define a Car class in models/car.py.

1.message: The class shell should be constructible with no arguments yet.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.title: Import Car and create one object
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.prompt: Keep `class Car:` with `pass` in `models/car.py`. In `main.py`, import `Car` from `models.car` and create one object with `car = Car()`. Do not add attributes or methods yet.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.hint: Use `from models.car import Car`, then call `Car()` to create one object.

help.concept: A class is a plan. Calling the class name with parentheses, like `Car()`, creates one object from that class.
help.hint_1: The import line belongs at the top of `main.py`.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.starterCode:
# Import the Car class.
# Then create one Car object.

main_py.content:
# Import the Car class.
# Then create one Car object.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.solutionCode:
from models.car import Car

car = Car()

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

car = Car()

1.message: Car should still be constructible with no arguments.

2.message: Create one Car object in main.py.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.title: Keep the model and main script separate
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.prompt: Keep the `Car` class in `models/car.py`. Keep `main.py` as the small script that imports the class and creates one object. Add `print("Car object ready")` in `main.py` so the program shows that it ran.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.hint: Do not move the class into `main.py`. Import it from the model file.

help.concept: A clean multifile project keeps model code in a model file and keeps the main script focused on using that model.
help.hint_2: `main.py` should import `Car`, create `car = Car()`, and print the ready message.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.starterCode:
from models.car import Car

car = Car()

# Print a short ready message below.

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

car = Car()

# Print a short ready message below.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.solutionCode:
from models.car import Car

car = Car()
print("Car object ready")

main_py.content:
from models.car import Car

car = Car()
print("Car object ready")

0.message: Keep the Car class defined in the model file.

1.message: Car should still be constructible with no arguments.

2.message: Create one Car object in main.py.

3.message: Print one short ready message from main.py.

mc-class-files-and-instances-class-role.title: What does a class do?
mc-class-files-and-instances-class-role.prompt: Which statements correctly describe a class at this point? Choose all that apply.
mc-class-files-and-instances-class-role.hint: Think of the class as the plan, not one finished object.

help.concept: A class is a reusable plan for making objects. One class can be used to create many objects.
help.hint_1: Look for choices about plans and object creation.
help.hint_2: Reject choices that say a class must immediately have many methods.

options.a: A class is a reusable plan for creating objects.
options.b: A class is always the same thing as one object.
options.c: Calling a class, such as `Car()`, creates an object.
options.d: A class must have a constructor before Python accepts it.

sc-class-files-and-instances-pass-meanin.title: Why use pass?
sc-class-files-and-instances-pass-meanin.prompt: Why can an empty class use `pass`?
sc-class-files-and-instances-pass-meanin.hint: `pass` lets Python keep a block valid even when there is no real code yet.

help.concept: `pass` is a placeholder. It keeps an empty class body syntactically valid until you add real code later.
help.hint_1: A class body cannot be completely empty.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about a placeholder.

options.b: `pass` is a placeholder that keeps the empty class valid.

fb-class-files-and-instances-import-name.title: Complete the import
fb-class-files-and-instances-import-name.prompt: Fill in the class name imported from `models.car`.
fb-class-files-and-instances-import-name.hint: Use the exact class name from `class Car:`.

help.concept: When a class is defined in another file, `main.py` imports the class by name.
help.hint_1: Class names use capital letters by convention.

dr-class-files-and-instances-order.title: Order the first class workflow
dr-class-files-and-instances-order.prompt: Put the steps in the order you follow when creating a class in a model file.
dr-class-files-and-instances-order.hint: Start with the class file before importing it.

help.concept: A clean first workflow is: define the class, import it, create one object, then run main.py.
help.hint_1: You cannot import a class that has not been defined yet.
help.hint_2: Creating the object comes after the import.

sketch-0.title: Start with a class shell
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
A **class** is a reusable plan for making objects.

An **object** is one thing created from that class. Python also calls that object an **instance**.

For example, a `Book` class can be the plan, and `Book()` can create one book object.

The smallest class shell looks like this:

```python
class Book:
    pass
```

Follow the execution: - `class Book:` starts the class definition.
- `pass` means "nothing goes here yet."
- The class exists, even though it does not store values or do actions yet.

Starting with a shell keeps the first step simple. You learn how a class exists before adding a constructor, attributes, or methods.

A class file defines the reusable blueprint; each instance receives its own state at runtime. Keeping the model in its own file also creates a stable import boundary that later services and tests can depend on.

sketch-1.title: Create one object from the class
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
After a class exists, you can create an object from it.

```python
class Book:
    pass

book = Book()
```

`Book` is the class.

`Book()` creates one object from that class.

`book` is the variable that stores the object.

This is the core class/object pattern:

```text
Class plan  ->  call the class  ->  object stored in a variable
```

You will use this pattern again when the class becomes more useful.

A class file defines the reusable blueprint; each instance receives its own state at runtime. Keeping the model in its own file also creates a stable import boundary that later services and tests can depend on.

sketch-2.title: Keep the class in its own file
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Real projects usually separate the class from the script that uses it.

The class can live in a model file:

```python
# models/book.py
class Book:
    pass
```

Then `main.py` imports and uses it:

```python
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book()
```

This keeps the project easier to read:

- `models/book.py` defines what a book is.
- `main.py` creates and uses book objects.

That separation matters more as the class grows.

A class file defines the reusable blueprint; each instance receives its own state at runtime. Keeping the model in its own file also creates a stable import boundary that later services and tests can depend on.
